content:
A Double-Edged Sword

2 min read
Just now

--

JavaScript is famous for being one of the most flexible and forgiving programming languages. This leniency makes it easy for beginners to get started and allows rapid prototyping, but it also introduces quirks that can lead to subtle bugs. Compared to stricter languages like Python, Rust, or TypeScript, JavaScript often bends the rules. Let’s explore some of its most forgiving features.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

JavaScript Syntaxes, a double edged sword!ASI: Automatic Semicolon Insertion
JavaScript inserts semicolon for you, even when you don’t write them. Consider the following example.
console.log("Hello")
console.log("World") // Works finereturn
{ ok: true } // Returns undefined! (ASI puts ; after return)
It’s convenient. But it could be misleading sometimes!
Trailing Commas Everywhere
Trailing commas are allowed in arrays, objects, function parameters, and imports.
const obj = {
a: 1,
b: 2, // trailing comma OK
};const arr = [1, 2, 3,]; // OK
function foo(a, b,) {} // OK
Loose Equality and Type Coercion
The == operator aggressively coerces types.
"5" == 5 // true
"" == 0 // true
[] == false // true
null == undefined // truePS: Use === if you find this behaviour problematic.
Optional var and Implicit Globals
In non-strict mode, assigning to an undeclared variable creates a global variable.
x = 42; // Creates global variable
function test() {
y = 10; // Also global if no 'let/const/var'
}Flexible Object and Function Syntax
JavaScript allows shorthand and variations in syntax.
const obj = { name: "Keerti", MediumProfile: "https://medium.com/@keertikotaru"};
const double = x => x * 2;!function(){}() // IIFE variation
(function(){})() // Another variation
for…in on Arrays
Iterating with for...in yields string indexes instead of values.
const arr = [10, 20, 30];
for (let i in arr) {
console.log(i); // "0", "1", "2"
}Dangling Commas and Sparse Arrays
Extra commas and whitespace are often ignored.
const a = [1, 2, , , 5]; // Sparse array with holes
const b = {,,}; // Valid empty object in some contextsReserved Words as Property Names
Reserved keywords can still be used as object keys.
const obj = {
class: "CS101",
delete: true,
function: () => {}
};The with Statement
In non-strict mode, with extends scope.
with (Math) {
console.log(PI); // Works, but deprecated
}Octal Literals with Leading Zero
Legacy octal literals are still recognized in non-strict mode.
const num = 010; // Treated as octal (8)Summary
JavaScript’s forgiving nature is both a blessing and a curse. It enables quick experimentation and rapid development but can also hide subtle bugs. This is why use strict;and TypeScript exist — to enforce stricter rules and make JavaScript safer when reliability matters.
